pattern: Splitting a signed stream into its two order-preserving sub-sequences and weaving them back together one-for-one turns a rearrangement puzzle into a simple merge.
hints: Since positives and negatives must each keep their own original relative order, think about collecting them separately first rather than trying to rearrange in place.
Once you have the positives in order and the negatives in order, the final array is just those two lists interleaved, positive then negative, over and over.

You can do this in one pass and O(1) extra bookkeeping by writing straight into a result array: keep a pointer starting at index 0 that advances by two for every positive you see, and a second pointer starting at index 1 that advances by two for every negative.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible input, one of each sign, negative given first
several of each sign already scattered
all negatives listed before any positive, forcing a real separation rather than an adjacent-swap fix
repeated magnitudes with opposite signs, so order must be tracked by position and not by value
values at the maximum allowed magnitude
one sign has a long run before switching, exercising the interleave pointer advancing several steps between switches
positives and negatives alternate in input already, so a lazy solution might skip the actual separation step and still look right by accident

stress.description: random values with a guaranteed equal positive/negative split and a shuffled arrangement
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size - (size % 2))
    half = n // 2
    pos_vals = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(half)]
    neg_vals = [-rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(half)]
    tags = [1] * half + [-1] * half
    rng.shuffle(tags)
    nums = []
    pi = ni = 0
    for t in tags:
        if t == 1:
            nums.append(pos_vals[pi])
            pi += 1
        else:
            nums.append(neg_vals[ni])
            ni += 1
    return [nums]
stress.note: tags are shuffled independently of the two value pools so the equal-count invariant always holds regardless of arrangement
